title: The Brainstem
subtitle: Recognise the three parts of the brainstem and why they matter clinically.

backPoints: The brainstem sits at the base of the brain.
It connects the cerebrum with the spinal cord.
The cerebellum lies behind it.
It carries many pathways travelling between the brain and the body.
cards.memoryLine: The brainstem connects the brain to the spinal cord.

cards.frontPrompt: Can you identify the whole brainstem?
backPoints: The brainstem is made up of the midbrain, pons and medulla.
These parts work together rather than acting as separate structures.
The brainstem helps control consciousness, breathing and cardiovascular function.
Many cranial nerve pathways also pass through it.

cards.frontPrompt: Which part sits at the top of the brainstem?
backPoints: The midbrain is the upper part of the brainstem.
It contains pathways involved in pupil responses and eye movements.
It also contributes to alertness and consciousness.
Midbrain dysfunction may therefore affect pupils, eye movement or conscious level.

cards.frontPrompt: Which part forms the rounded bulge?
backPoints: The pons is the rounded middle part of the brainstem.
Its name means bridge.
It links important pathways between the cerebrum, cerebellum and medulla.
It also contributes to the control and pattern of breathing.
cards.memoryLine: Pons: the bridge and the bulge.

cards.frontPrompt: Which part continues into the spinal cord?
backPoints: The medulla is the lowest part of the brainstem.
It continues into the spinal cord.
It contains centres involved in breathing, heart rate and blood pressure.
Severe medullary dysfunction can rapidly become life-threatening.
cards.memoryLine: Medulla: breathing, heart rate and blood pressure.

cards.frontPrompt: Why can brainstem injury affect consciousness?
backPoints: Brainstem networks help maintain arousal and wakefulness.
Damage or compression can reduce a person's level of consciousness.
A falling conscious level may therefore indicate serious neurological deterioration.
Always assess the whole clinical picture and escalate new changes.
cards.memoryLine: The brainstem helps keep the brain awake.

cards.frontPrompt: What changes might suggest brainstem dysfunction?
backPoints: Possible signs include a reduced conscious level.
Pupil or eye movement abnormalities may occur.
Breathing pattern, heart rate or blood pressure may change.
New brainstem signs require urgent assessment and escalation.
cards.memoryLine: Consciousness, pupils, breathing and circulation may all change.

quiz.question: Which three structures make up the brainstem?
Which part is at the top of the brainstem?
Which part of the brainstem forms the obvious rounded bulge?
Which part of the brainstem continues into the spinal cord?
Which brainstem region is most closely associated with pupil pathways and eye movements?
The word pons is commonly remembered as meaning:
Which functions are strongly associated with the medulla?
options: Breathing, heart rate and blood pressure

quiz.question: Which combination may indicate brainstem dysfunction?
options: Reduced consciousness, abnormal pupils and altered breathing
